comments:
Summary
This paper proposes FTS-BRNN, a bidirectional RNN model that applies supervision at every time step ("full-time supervision") for factoid question answering (quiz-bowl style). The model uses GRU units and an output layer combining forward and backward hidden states; training minimizes a margin loss at each time step using the correct answer and wrong answers. The paper presents ablation experiments comparing full-time supervision vs pooling-based supervision, GRU vs LSTM, and compares to baselines (BOW, BOW-DT, QANTA, QA-LSTM) on two subsets (Literature, History) of a quiz-bowl dataset and reports improved accuracy.

Strengths
- The idea of applying supervision at every time step in BRNN is simple and well motivated; empirical ablation shows notable gains over pooling-based supervision on the evaluated benchmarks. 
- The paper includes focused comparisons (ablation for output layer, loss choice, GRU vs LSTM) and compares to several prior baselines including recursive NN (QANTA) and LSTM-based BRNN (QA-LSTM).
- Results indicate improved accuracy on the used factoid QA subsets.

Weaknesses and Concerns
- Novelty is incremental. The main technical novelty is applying a per-time-step margin loss in BRNN; similar ideas have appeared in related work (the authors cite [9]) and the distinction from prior art is not strongly argued beyond empirical comparison. 
- Experiments are limited in scope: only two (relatively small) subsets from one dataset are used. It is unclear how well the approach scales to larger, more diverse QA datasets or to non-factoid QA. 
- The paper lacks several important empirical details/analyses: no statistical significance testing is reported; there is no runtime or memory analysis (the full-time margin loss using all wrong answers may be computationally costly); no ablation on negative sampling strategies (they state they use all wrong answers but do not study sampled negatives). 
- Comparison to more recent attention-based and other strong RNN/Transformer-style baselines is missing (QA-LSTM is included but attention models are only cited). This weakens claims of state-of-the-art more broadly.
- Presentation has issues: the provided text includes repeated sentences and some formatting/clarity problems. Certain hyperparameters (e.g., dropout 0.7) are surprising and not justified; more training details would help reproducibility. 
- Error analysis and qualitative examples are absent; it is unclear in which cases full-time supervision helps or fails.

Recommendations for authors
- Expand experimental evaluation: include larger/more diverse datasets, compare to attention-based and other recent baselines, and provide significance tests. 
- Report computational cost and memory usage; study using sampled negatives vs all negatives for the margin loss. 
- Provide qualitative/error analysis to explain where per-time-step supervision helps. 
- Clean up presentation, remove repeated text, and add missing implementation/training details for reproducibility.

Overall assessment
The paper presents a straightforward and practically useful empirical finding (full-time supervision at BRNN time steps improves performance on the evaluated factoid QA tasks) and supports it with ablation experiments. However, the contribution is incremental, experiments are narrowly scoped, and some methodological/analysis details are missing. I therefore view the work as moderate interest to the community: suitable for publication as a short/poster track contribution after revision and expanded evaluation.
